Name: rate-distortion_based_model_compression
Title: Exploring Rate-Distortion Bounds in Model Compression for Generalization Improvement
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that by systematically applying rate-distortion theory to model compression, we can improve generalization performance by optimizing the compression rate relative to task-relevant information.
Related Work: Recent studies have demonstrated that model compression can enhance population risk and generalization error, but they often lack a systematic framework that connects these improvements to rate-distortion theory. Our proposal aims to fill this gap by explicitly linking compression strategies to achievable rate-distortion bounds, thereby providing a clearer understanding of the trade-offs involved.
Abstract: Model compression techniques have gained prominence in machine learning, with evidence suggesting that compressed models can outperform their uncompressed counterparts in terms of generalization. However, the underlying mechanisms remain inadequately understood. In this work, we propose a novel framework that leverages rate-distortion theory to systematically analyze model compression strategies in the context of generalization. We will derive achievable rate-distortion bounds for various compression methods and explore how these bounds correlate with the generalization performance of the models. By treating model compression as a rate-distortion problem, we aim to provide a clearer operational understanding of how task-relevant information can be preserved while reducing model complexity. We will conduct experiments that demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach across different architectures and datasets, establishing a solid theoretical foundation for future research in the field.
Experiments: Conduct experiments on different neural network architectures (e.g., CNNs and Transformers) using various compression techniques (e.g., pruning, quantization) to evaluate the impact on generalization performance.
Measure the mutual information between the model's parameters and its outputs to determine task-relevant information preserved post-compression.
Analyze the rate-distortion curves for each compression technique to identify the optimal compression rates that minimize generalization error.
Use cross-validation to assess generalization performance, tracking metrics such as accuracy and loss on held-out datasets.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The complexity of deriving precise rate-distortion bounds may limit the applicability of the framework to specific cases.
Different datasets may exhibit varying levels of compressibility, potentially impacting the generalization improvements observed.
The experiments may require careful tuning of hyperparameters for each model and dataset, making reproducibility a challenge.
